pull.title: cmake: Recommend native CMake commands in README
pull.body:
Resolves one item in #1235. Closes #1294.

cc @hebasto

repo.name: secp256k1
repo.description: Optimized C library for EC operations on curve secp256k1

repo.name: secp256k1
repo.description: Optimized C library for EC operations on curve secp256k1

events.message:
cmake: Recommend native CMake commands in README

Resolves one item in #1235. Closes #1294.

comments.diff_hunk:
@@ -79,9 +79,9 @@ To maintain a pristine source tree, CMake encourages to perform an out-of-source
 
     $ mkdir build && cd build
     $ cmake ..

@@ -79,9 +79,9 @@ To maintain a pristine source tree, CMake encourages to perform an out-of-source
 
     $ mkdir build && cd build
     $ cmake ..
-    $ make
-    $ make check  # run the test suite
-    $ sudo make install  # optional
+    $ cmake --build .
+    $ ctest  # run the test suite
+    $ sudo cmake --build . --target install  # optional
comments.body:
Is it worth mentioning a modern and more effective alternative, which is available for CMake >= 3.15:
```
$ sudo cmake --install .  # optional
```
?

comments.diff_hunk:
@@ -79,9 +79,9 @@ To maintain a pristine source tree, CMake encourages to perform an out-of-source
 
     $ mkdir build && cd build
     $ cmake ..
comments.body: Hm, yeah, I think shorter is better, and omitting the -S is also more common. (See the official tutorial https://cmake.org/cmake/help/latest/guide/tutorial/A%20Basic%20Starting%20Point.html for example.)

comments.diff_hunk:
@@ -79,9 +79,9 @@ To maintain a pristine source tree, CMake encourages to perform an out-of-source
 
     $ mkdir build && cd build
     $ cmake ..
-    $ make
-    $ make check  # run the test suite
-    $ sudo make install  # optional
+    $ cmake --build .
+    $ ctest  # run the test suite
+    $ sudo cmake --build . --target install  # optional
comments.body: Thanks for the suggestion. In fact, I had this first, but I changed to the current form because we still support CMake 3.13.
